name: Langsuir
description: The Langsuir is a vengeful female spirit in Malaysian folklore, originating from a woman who died during childbirth. She is depicted as a beautiful woman with long, flowing hair that covers a hole in the back of her neck from which she sucks the blood of victims, particularly newborns and young children. Her long nails are sharp and dangerous, and she can transform into an owl, further enabling her to hunt at night. The Langsuir is often associated with feelings of loss, injustice, and unfulfilled potential, embodying the tragic fate of a woman denied the joys of motherhood. Protective measures are often taken to prevent a woman who died during childbirth from becoming a Langsuir, including placing glass beads in her mouth and needles in her palms to prevent her spirit from wandering. Her cries are said to resemble the mournful hooting of an owl, a chilling harbinger of her presence. She is a fearsome figure, embodying the dangers lurking in the shadows and the pain of a life cut short. The Langsuir's existence serves as a cautionary tale and a reminder of the importance of respecting the delicate balance of life and death.
subtitle: Vengeful spirit of childbirth death
appearance: A beautiful woman with long, black, flowing hair that conceals a hole in the back of her neck. She has long, sharp nails and piercing red eyes. Sometimes appears as an owl with long hair.
habitat: Dark forests, abandoned buildings, near graveyards, and areas where a woman has died during childbirth.
behavior: The Langsuir seeks revenge for her untimely death and the loss of her child. She preys on newborns, young children, and men, often luring them with her beauty before attacking. She is said to laugh while feeding on blood and is easily angered by disrespect or mockery.
weaknesses: Specific rituals to bind her spirit

keyMyths.title: Origin of the Langsuir
keyMyths.description: A woman dies during childbirth, often due to neglect or mistreatment. Her spirit, filled with rage and sorrow, transforms into the Langsuir to exact revenge on the living, especially those responsible for her death or the loss of her child.

keyMyths.title: Langsuir and the Village
keyMyths.description: A Langsuir terrorizes a village, killing newborns and causing illness. A skilled bomoh (shaman) is called upon to banish or bind the spirit, often using iron objects, prayers, and specific rituals to protect the community.

sources.author: Richard Winstedt
sources.description: A classic text on Malay folklore and magic, detailing various beliefs and practices related to spirits and supernatural beings.
sources.text: A classic text on Malay folklore and magic, detailing various beliefs and practices related to spirits and supernatural beings.

sources.author: Rosemary Ellen Guiley
sources.work: The Encyclopedia of Demons and Demonology
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of demons and related entities from various cultures, including a brief entry on the Langsuir.
